serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of stir-fried or blanched broccoli is approximately 150 g, typically served as a vegetable accompaniment alongside rice or noodles in a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (SR Legacy ID 11091)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy cooked broccoli values used as the primary nutrient anchor. Sodium value elevated to 233 mg/100g to reflect added soy sauce and seasoning typical of Singaporean stir-fried or blanched broccoli preparation, consistent with the input record. All other macros cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database for cooked broccoli. Values are consistent with the input record and are accepted as plausible for a lightly seasoned cooked broccoli dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Broccoli is a non-starchy cruciferous vegetable with very low digestible carbohydrate content. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) consistently report GI values for broccoli in the range of 10–15. A GI of 15 is used here as a conservative low estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 5.33 g carbohydrate × 1.5) / 100 = 1.2 g, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 233 mg/100g from added soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce and minimal oyster sauce when ordering this dish, as the seasoning contributes the majority of the 233 mg/100g sodium load; the intrinsic broccoli potassium (~293 mg/100g) provides a partial DASH-diet offset but does not fully neutralise the added sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (15), low digestible carbohydrate (5.3 g/100g), and 2.7 g/100g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried or blanched broccoli is an excellent choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index of 15, low sugar content of 1.3 g/100g, and 2.7 g/100g dietary fibre which slows glucose absorption; patients should request minimal oyster sauce to avoid added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.33 g/100g), zero trans fat, high vitamin C (66.7 mg/100g), and anti-inflammatory sulforaphane from broccoli
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Broccoli prepared with minimal oil is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat (0.33 g/100g), zero trans fat, high antioxidant vitamin C (66.7 mg/100g), and sulforaphane content; patients should limit soy sauce additions to manage sodium intake and choose sesame oil sparingly to keep total fat moderate.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory sulforaphane, high vitamin C antioxidant activity, negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Broccoli is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to sulforaphane's anti-inflammatory properties and high vitamin C (66.7 mg/100g) antioxidant activity; use low-sodium soy sauce to avoid sodium-driven tissue swelling and request minimal oyster sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried or blanched broccoli (西兰花) is a simple, nutritious vegetable side dish widely served in Singaporean Chinese hawker centres, coffee shops, and casual restaurants. The dish consists of fresh broccoli florets that are either briefly blanched in boiling water or stir-fried in a wok with garlic, a small amount of oil (typically sesame or vegetable oil), light soy sauce, and optionally oyster sauce. The result is tender-crisp florets with a mild, slightly sweet, earthy flavour and a vibrant green colour. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 53 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 2.7 g fat, 5.3 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g dietary fibre, 1.3 g sugar, and 233 mg sodium. Vitamin C content is notably high at 66.7 mg/100g. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making this dish suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension (with low-sodium sauce), and cardiovascular disease. Broccoli contains sulforaphane, a bioactive compound with anti-inflammatory and potential anti-cancer properties. This dish is vegetarian and halal-friendly. A standard side dish portion is approximately 150 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried broccoli?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli data and HPB Singapore database for cooked broccoli. Sodium is elevated above plain cooked broccoli to reflect typical Singaporean seasoning (soy sauce, oyster sauce), which is a reasonable adaptation. No exact dish-level entry for this specific preparation exists in a single approved source, hence medium confidence rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this lightly seasoned cooked broccoli preparation is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo database at the dish level as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in the primary source consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific dish preparation in the primary approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked broccoli with minimal added oil. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the base preparation. The cultural.primary_cuisine field in the input ('Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok') appears to be a vendor/restaurant name rather than a cuisine classification and has been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese'. Potassium value of 293 mg/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli (SR Legacy ID 11091).
